---
title: "Thematic accounts - land take extent accounts in recreation areas"
subtitle: |
  [ENCREC_001] (Extent accounts of land take in all recreation areas) \
  [ENCREC_002] (Extent accounts of land take in very important (A) and important (B) recreation areas) \
  [ENCREC_003] (Extent accounts of all recreation areas) \
  [ENCREC_004] (Extent accounts of very important (A) and important (B) recreation areas) \
  [ENCREC_005] (Extent accounts of specially important nature lost due to land take in all recreation areas) \
  [ENCREC_006] (Extent accounts of specially important nature lost due to land take in very important (A) and important (B) recreation areas)
# delete the line with the second indicatorID (line 5) 
# if it's not relevant for you.
# In the parentheses you can add a word to distinguish multiple indicators 
# from each other, for example the ecosystem type that they cover
indicatorID: 
  - ENCREC_001
  - ENCREC_002
  - ENCREC_003
  - ENCREC_004
  - ENCREC_005
  - ENCREC_006 # delete whole line if not needed
# If you are presenting multiple indicators, and have multiple 
# indicator IDs, then add them as a list here, and make sure you add
# the same number of list entries, 
# and in the same order that corresponds to the indicator IDs,  
# to the meta data fields where the indicators differ. 
# For example, if the indicators cover different ecosystem types, 
# add the ecosystems as a list under 'Ecosystems', but if they are in the
# same realm, same country etc., then you only need to write the realm and
# country once. 
indicatorName: 
  - Extent accounts of land take in all recreation areas # add more by adding another line starting '-'
  - Extent accounts of land take in very important (A) and important (B) recreation areas
  - Extent accounts of all recreation areas
  - Extent accounts of very important (A) and important (B) recreation areas
  - Extent accounts of specially important nature lost due to land take in all recreation areas
  - Extent accounts of specially important nature lost due to land take in very important (A) and important (B) recreation areas
folderName: code # name of the folder under 'indicators/'. 
# If it's a single indicator being reported, the folder name should be the same as the indicatorID. 
# If there are multiple ID's then the folder name could look something like XX_YYY_ZZZ-ÅÅÅ.
country: Norway
continent: Europe
# ECT: write ECT here # e.g. A2 - Chemical State characteristics
Realm: 
  - all terrestrial  # e.g. Freshwater (F)
Biome: 
  - all terrestrial  # e.g. F1 Rivers and streams biome
Ecosystem: 
  - all terrestrial  # e.g. F1.3 Freeze-thaw streams
yearAdded: 2026 # the year when the indicator was first submitted to ecRxiv
yearLastUpdate: 2026
status: complete # options are complete, incomplete, or deprecated
Version: "000.001" # Beta-versions are numbers incrementally with values below 0. Operational versions can be given the version numbers above 0. Use this terms similar as for software versioning,
VersionComment: This is the first version of the thematic land take extent accounts for outdoor recreation areas being compiled. # Describe briefly how this version differs from earlier versions 
url: https://github.com/NINAnor/recereation_encroachment_accounts
# Leave the url as is, but replace XX_YYYY_ZZ with the name of the root folder for the current indicator(s). It should be the same as the indicatorID, but if there are more indicator IDs presented in the same folder, the folder name might be different, e.g. XX_YYYY_AAA-BBB 
Normalised: No normalisation, except when ENCACC_002 and ENCREC_006 are presented as percentages. In that case we normalised by the total area of the municiplaities / counties (ENCREC_002); or the total area of land take whithin municipalities / counties (ENCREC_006). # Yes or no depending on whether the metric has been normliased or not.
SpatialAggregationPathway: Area of land take calculated at polygon level, then aggregated at municipal level, then aggregated at county and national levels. Standard or cumulative sums were used. # e.g. Scale - Transform - Aggregate (length-weighted arithmetic mean) - Truncate
format: 
  html:
    embed-resources: true
    code-fold: true
    toc: true
    toc-title: Contents
    toc-depth: 3
    smooth-scroll: true
execute: 
  cache: true
author:
  - name: Sylvie Clappe             # Enter name
    email: sylvie.clappe@nina.no  # Enter email
    affiliations:
      - id: myID
        name: Norwegian Institute for Nature Research  # Enter affiliations
  - name: David Barton              #  Enter subsequent authors like this, or remove if not relevant
    affiliations:
      - ref: myID               # To reuse affiliations reference the id like this
  - name: Reto Spielhofer             #  Enter subsequent authors like this, or remove if not relevant
    affiliations:
      - ref: myID 
  - name: Zander Venter             #  Enter subsequent authors like this, or remove if not relevant
    affiliations:
      - ref: myID 
AuthorList: Clappe, S., Barton, D.N., Spielhofer, R.  
date: March 04, 2026 # Enter date
data_availability: none
code_reproducibility: none
open_science_badge: none
callout-icon: false
lightbox: true
css: ../../../style.css
code-links:
      - text: Add a review
        icon: github
        href: https://github.com/NINAnor/ecRxiv
hide: TRUE # Change to FALSE
---

<!--# This is a template for how to document the indicator analyses. Make sure also to not change the order, or modify, the headers, unless you really need to. This is because it easier to read if all the indicators are presented using the same layout. If there is one header where you don't have anything to write, just leave the header as is, and don't write anything below it. If you are providing code, be careful to annotate and comment on every step in the analysis. Before starting it is recommended to fill in as much as you can in the metadata file. This file will populate the initial table in your output.-->

<!--# Load all you dependencies here -->

```{r setup}
#| include: false
#| cache: false
library(knitr)
library(tidyverse)
library(kableExtra)
library(here)
library(anybadger)
library(yaml)
library(tibble)
library(conflicted)
library(httr2)
library(giscoR)
library(zen4R)
library(jsonlite)
library(sf)
library(purrr)
library(numform)
library(DT)
library(tmap)

# weird workarounds for cache issues
conflicts_prefer(dplyr::filter)
conflicts_prefer(dplyr::lag)
conflicts_prefer(dplyr::select)
pull<-dplyr::pull

knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
knitr::opts_chunk$set(warning = FALSE, message = FALSE)

# Remove dplyr and messages
options(dplyr.summarise.inform = FALSE)
```

```{r source}
#| echo: false
#| cache: false
source(here::here("_common.R"))
```

```{r working_directory}
#| include: false
#| cache: false

# Workaround to force the working directory
# Needs to be set after {r source} otherwise
# {_common.R is not found}
opts_knit$set(root.dir = here:::here())
```


<!--# The following parts are autogenerated. Do not edit. -->

::: {layout-ncol="5"}

```{r statusBadge}
#| echo: false
status_badge(as.character(st))
```

```{r versionBadge}
#| echo: false
version_badge(my_version_number = as.character(version[[1]]),
  folder_name = folder_name)
```

```{r dataBadge}
#| echo: false
data_badge(as.character(badges[[1]]))
```

```{r codeBadge}
#| echo: false
code_badge(as.character(badges[[2]]))
```

```{r openScienceBadge}
#| echo: false
open_science_badge(as.character(badges[[3]]))
```
::: 
 

> **Recommended citation**: `r paste(auth, " ", year, ". ", name, " (ID(s): ", folder_name, ") ", "v. ", version, ". ", url, sep="")`



::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## Metadata
```{r tbl-meta2}
#| tbl-cap: 'Indicator metadata'
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
meta |>
  dplyr::filter(Variable %in% c(
    "Indicator ID",
    "Indicator Name",
    "Continent",
    "Country",
    "Ecosystem Condition Typology Class", 
    "Realm",
    "Biome",
    "Ecosystem",
    "Year added", 
    "Last update",
    "Version",
    "Version comment",
    "Normalised",
    "Spatial aggregation pathway")) |>   
  kable()

```
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}

## Log

<!--# Update this logg with short messages for each update -->
- 25 February 2026 - Analyses completed
- 04 March 2026 - First version
:::


<hr />

<!--# Document you work below.  -->

## 1. Summary

<!--# 
With a maximum of 300 words, describe the indicator in general terms as you would to a non-expert. Think of this as a kind of common language summary. Follow the four numbers sub-headers below. under point 2, it is possible under  include a bullet point list of the specific steps in the workflow. 
 -->

1. **Background and rationale**:

Norway is losing nature at high speed. According to the NRK article “Norge i Rødt, Hvitt og Grått”, Norway lost 208 km2 of semi-natural lands to artificial land expansion; a process known as land take (NRK 2024; Venter et al., 2024). Almost a year later, in October 2025, NRK released another article looking at the rate of land take in Europe, «Europe from Green to Grey – Norway has the highest loss of nature per capita in Europe». In this article, Norway had the alarming rate of more than  38 m2 of nature loss per inhabitant in the period 2018-2024 (NRK, 2025a). 

Outdoor recreation areas are one of the foundations of people’s connection with nature, which is an important part of the Norwegian culture and lifestyle. In Norway some municipalities have mapped their outdoor recreation areas for almost two decades. In this report, we decided to explore how much of this nature loss happened in outdoor recreation areas and what type of nature was lost.

In this document, we explore how much of this nature loss happened in outdoor recreation areas of both counties and municipalities and what type of nature was lost. To be relevant in a planning context, we also focused on the nature types referred to Environmental Administration's Objection Practice under the collective term «nature of particular importance».


2. **Methods**:

We created extent accounts for land take within the county and municipalities as a whole. Four main datasets were used to create our accounts: (i) a map of municipal administrative boundaries from Eurostat; (ii) a map of outdoor recreation areas from the Norwegian Environmental Agency; (iii) maps of built-up expansion over Norway 2017-2014 from Venter 2025;  and (iv) a map of valued nature types from the Norwegian Environmental Agency. The analyses mostly relied on various spatial intersection of the data. 

We also developed a simple traffic-light system to inform users of the uncertainty of our results. This system is mainly based on the user accuracy of the prediction of land take, and the temporal discrepancy between datasets.

3. **Main headlines**:

- More than 13 000 football pitches (97 000 decares; 97 km2) of nature disappeared due to land take between 218 and 2024. More than 80% (78 000 decares) occurred in important and very important outdoor recreation areas.

- With annual losses of more than 2 200 and 1 800 decares respectively, Buskerud and Innlandet lost the biggest area of their important and very important outdoor rec-reation areas to land take.

- Over 1 600 decares of specially important nature disappeared in important and very important outdoor recreation areas due to land take.

- With 11,5%, Roagaland has the highest percentage of land take built on specially important nature of its important and very important outdoor recreation areas. While less than 1% of the nature lost to land take in the recreation areas of Troms, Oslo and Innlandet was specially important.


## 2. About the underlying data

- **DT01**: [Local Administrative Units](^https://ec.europa.eu/eurostat/web/gisco/geodata/statistical-units/local-administrative-units$): Map of the administrative boundaries of municipalities in Norway. It is produced by Eurostat. We chose not to use the Kartverket map of the municipalities as their boundaries extend to the marine habitats. Eurostat’s boundaries are restricted to the terrestrial habitats. As DT02 is limited to terrestrial habitats, we chose to restrict the extent of outdoor recreation areas to terrestrial habitats only.

- **DT02**: Friluftslivsom-råder - kartlagte](^https://kartkatalog.geonorge.no/metadata/friluftslivsomraader-kartlagte/91e31bb7-356f-4478-bcba-d5c2de6e91bc$): Map of the area and values of municipal outdoor recreation areas. The mapping has been done by municipalities themselves in accordance with Miljødirektoratets Guidance M98-2013 (Tidligere DN håndbok 25 – 2004 Kartlegging og verdsetting av friluftslivsområder).

- **DT03**: [Map of built-up expansion ("nedbygging") over Norway 2017-2024 with year of loss, Venter (2025)](^https://zenodo.org/records/15130992$): Map of potential “nedbygging” (i.e., land take) event in Norway over 2018-2024. This data have been using Sentinal-2 satellite imagery and  a convolutional neural network from Google called Dynamic World.

- **DT04**: [Naturtyper - verdsatte](^https://kartkatalog.geonorge.no/metadata/naturtyper-verdsatte/64cbb884-a19d-4356-a114-380cfe4a7314$): Map of the habitats considered as specially important nature  in accordance with the valuation criteria in guideline M-1941 Impact assessments for climate and the environment.

<!--# Give a general introduction the datasets you have used, and provide more specific details in the sub-chapters.  -->

### 2.1 Spatial and temporal resolution and extent

- **DT01**: vector data last updated in 2025 and covering 2024 administrative boundaries. DT01 covers all of Norway's municipalities.

- **DT02**: vector data last updated in 2025 and covering the land take that appeared between 2008 and 2025. DT02 has a not national coverage. Only the municipalities which mapped their outdoor recreation areas are represented: 300 (80%).

- **DT03**: vector data last updated in 2025 and covering the land take that appeared between 2018 and 2024. DT02 has a national coverage.

- **DT04**: vector data last updated in 2025 and whose mapping years of habitats range between 1899 and 2025. DT03 does not have a national coverage (see bias below).


### 2.2 Sampling design

- **DT01**: Not applicable

- **DT02**: Municipalities map their outdoor recreation areas themselves following Miljødirektoratet's Guidance M98-2013. Despite offering a standard to map these areas, there is a disparity among municipalities on how this mapping is being conducted and quality-insured.

- **DT03**: detection of encroachment is based on Sentinel-2 data and a convulotional neural network from Google called Dynamic World. These data are associated with a 85% user accuracy, this means that the detected encroachment are incorrect 18% of the time. This accuracy however varies depending on the year of detection of the land take: 80.6% for 2018; 81.8 for 2019; 82.2 for 2020; 84% for 2021; 86% for 2022; 95% for 2023. 2024 is not reported, we will assume it is equal to the overall user accuracy of 82%.

- **DT03**: No national coverage or revisit or sites. This means two things: (i) that if a habitat is not mapped at a particular location, we do not know if it's because it is indeed not there or that it simply hasn't been mapped yet; (ii) once a habitat has been mapped, there is no temporal indication for ensuring it is still there over the years.

### 2.3 Original units

- **DT01**: Municipalities

- **DT02**: Municipalities

- **DT03**: Land take polygons

- **DT04**: Habitats polygons


### 2.3 Instructions for citing, using and accessing data

- **DT01**: [Acces here](^https://ec.europa.eu/eurostat/web/gisco/geodata/statistical-units/local-administrative-units$). Can be cited as: Eurostat (2025). Local Administrative Units (LAU) 2024. 

- **DT02**: [Access here](^https://kartkatalog.geonorge.no/metadata/friluftslivsomraader-kartlagte/91e31bb7-356f-4478-bcba-d5c2de6e91bc$). Can be cited as: Miljødirektoratet (2025). Friluftslivsom-råder - kartlagte 2025. 

- **DT03**: [Access here](^https://zenodo.org/records/15130992$). Can be cited as: Venter, Z. (2025). Map of built-up expansion ("nedbygging") over Norway 2017-2024 with year of loss [Data set]. Zenodo. https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.15130992

- **DT04**: [Access here](^https://kartkatalog.geonorge.no/metadata/naturtyper-verdsatte/64cbb884-a19d-4356-a114-380cfe4a7314$). Can be cited as: Miljødirektoratet (2025). Verdsatte naturtyper 2025. 

### 2.4 Additional comments about the dataset

- **DT01**: was used to delineate the administrative boundaries of the municipalities and counties.

- **DT02**: was used to delineate the boundaries of the municipal outdoor recreation areas.

- **DT03**: was used to identify the area of land take.

- **DT04**: was used to identify the area and type of specially important nature lost to land take.

## 3. Indicator properties

## 3. Indicator properties

### 3.1 Mis-alignment with the System of Environmental Economic Accounting - Ecosystem Accounting
For ENCACC_001, we have two deviations from the SEEA-EA traditional extent accounts:

1.	A land take represent a land change over time. The SEEA-EA uses an “ecosystem-based” approach referring to ecosystem types and assets, whereby we record the extent in the opening and closing year of the accounting period, as well as a net change. land take being already a change, the accounts that are created will be for a land change which is not an “ecosystem” asset  per se. Here, we however decided to consider the land change as an “ecosystem” and report an opening (2018) and closing (2024) extent for the land change. This led to a subsequent deviation regarding the calculation of a total change in land take extent during the accounting period (see point 2 below).

2. SEEA-EA extent accounts report the total area of an ecosystem during the accounting period and details the reductions, additions and net changes in this extent. As per point 1 above, land take intrinsically represent change in land cover only as the data only record the area of new land take in “natural areas” each year. Even though we consider it as a “pseudo-ecosystem” in our extent accounts, it meant that we could not calculate a change over the opening and closing years in the traditional way the SEEA-EA presents by subtracting the yearly opening extent to the yearly closing extent. To counteract this, we decided to also compile the cumulative area so we could compile a change in area between the opening and closing year. However, as previously noted, the land take data only record the apparition of new land take each year. This means that we only report the additions in area over the years, not the reductions (e.g., restoration works). In other words, we report when an encroachment appear but not when it disappears. There is thus a bias in the way the information is presented and the total change we present over the accounting period (2018-2024) is not a “net change”. Overall this means that we do not compile full SEEA-EA extent accounts, but only partial ones.

For ENCACC_002, we only presented the total area lost over the accounting period per habitat type. This was to simplify the interpretation by the municpalities as areas are not lost constantly over time.

### 3.2 Spatial aggregation

We first calculated at the municipal level, then aggregated at county and national level. 

When we present the ENCREC_001-002 map of the land take as a share of the total  municipal / county outdoor recreation area, we normalise the total land take area at the municipal / county level by the total area of outdoor recreation areas within the municipality / county. 

When we present the ENCACC_006 map of percentage of land take that was specially important nature, we normalise the total area of specially important nature lost to land take by the total area of land take occuring within the outdoor recreation areas of the municipality / county. 


## 5. Uncertainties
### 5.1 General uncertainties
There are several sources of uncertainty that underlay the results that are presented in this report. Among them, we identified four six sources:

1. **Prediction uncertainty linked to DT01**, whereby 18% of the land take areas are false positive. These means that 18% of the land take  areas are not “real” land take. 

2. **Discrepancy in temporal coverages between the datasets used (DT01, DT02, DT03)**. As already described previously, all the datasets used in our analysis do not have the same temporal coverage. This creates uncertainty when performing the spatial intersection as data are not representative from the same year. We took this source of uncertainty into account as described in section 5.2 Reported uncertainties. Note that we omitted DT02 as boundaries within municipalities are unlikely to change over time.

3. **Temporal disparity of the mapping year within DT02**. Outdoor recreation areas have not been mapped in the same year in all municipalities. The overall time period starts from 2008 and finishes in 2025. Most of them have been mapped and / or updated their mapping between 2014 and 2021. 104 municipalities (35%) mapped their outdoor recreation areas over several years, while 196 (75%) mapped their recreation areas over one single year. When municipalities mapped over several years, they added new polygons to either cover new areas or to extent their original boundaries. The temporal coverage of DT02 (2008-2025) does not correspond to the temporal coverage of DT03 (2018-2024). In our analyses, we decided to keep the entire temporal coverage of DT02 and did not sub-select outdoor recreation areas that matched the temporal coverage of DT03. The reasoning is based on the assumption that outdoor recreation areas are constant over time. This means two things: 

    - The boundaries of outdoor recreation areas do not change over time. As dis-cussed above with Tabell 3, municipalities simply add new polygons to the previously mapped recreation polygons if they want to update the boundaries of their outdoor recreation areas. So it is reasonable to consider the bounda-ries as constant overt time as their updates do not spatially overlap each other, they are simply additive.

    - Once mapped, an outdoor recreation area is assumed to always have been an outdoor recreation area, even before its mapping year. For example, an outdoor recreation area mapped in 2025 is assumed to also have been an outdoor recreation area in 2008. This means that if a land take is detected in 2021 but that the outdoor recreation area has been mapped in 2023, we still account this land take as happening in the area in 2021.

    Despite this assumption, we are conscious that outdoor recreation areas mapped in 2008 but that have not been updated since represent a source of uncertainty as we do not know if the absence of update in the outdoor recreation area boundaries is due to the fact that they really have not changed since 2008, or because the municipality didn’t have the resources to update them despite changes. This uncertainty is partially already taken into account by accounting for the temporal disparity be-tween datasets (point 2 above).



3. **Spatial quality of the boundaries of DT02**. The quality of the differ among municipalities with some mapping their outdoor recreation areas quite coarsely, while others detailed the boundaries very finely. This creates uncertainty regarding the area of the outdoor recreation are mapped which, if mapped coarsely, is bigger than what it is in reality. It was not possible to take into account such mapping uncertainty in our results as we could not find any appropriate proxy to represent it. We thus assumed that it was minor as improving the boundaries would likely not change the bigger trends presented in this report.


5. **Spatial quality of the boundaries of DT03**. It was not possible to assess the quality of the boundaries of the mapped specially important nature, neither the rate of error in the identification of the nature types. We are however conscious that they would impact the quality of the results if a habitat is mapped smaller or bigger than it is in reality, or if a habitat is not recorded as occurring due to misclassification in the habitat type. Due to the lack of potential proxies for these uncertainties, it was not possible to take them into account in our results.


5. **Spatial uncertainty linked to DT03**. As previously explained, this dataset doesn’t have a national coverage. This means that if a habitat is not recorded at a specific spatial location, it is either not occurring at that particular location, or occurring but the location has not been surveyed yet. Unfortunately, it was not possible to take into account this mapping uncertainty in our results as we could not find any appropriate proxy to represent it.



### 5.2 Reported uncertainties
Originally, for each municipality in each of the extent accounts we compiled, we calculated a qualitative indicator of uncertainty associated to the results. This indicator is based on the first two sources of uncertainty described in the previous section (i.e., prediction uncertainty of DT02 and temporal discrepancy between datasets) and has been designed as a traffic light system whereby:

-	Green: “Certain”
- Yellow: “Rather certain”
- Orange: “Rather uncertain”
- Red: “Uncertain”

Note that although we use “certain” in our Likert scale, no results can truly be qualified as “certain” with scientific robustness due to the numerous sources of uncertainty we listed previously. This was done to make the interpretation of our indicator more user-friendly.

The complete description of how this indicator has been compiled can be found in "Traffic_light_system_explanation" in .docx or pdf in the internal MAREA sharefolder. Due to several issues including the fact that we apply the national user accuracy to th epolygon level and that we would rather need a polygon-level accuracy. We coded this system but did not use it in our NINA Rapport 2754.

Also it was brought to our attention that areas at national level needed to be corrected using a design-based approach: area x PA/UA with PA producer accuracy and UA user accuracy. This is to quantify the uncertainty around the area compiled. This meant that our lookup table to define the traffic light needed to include PA. Unfortunately, once again, PA and UA for nedbygging data are only available at national level. Also is UA is available yearly, PA is only available over the entire accounting period 2018-2024. So we decided to keep the code for later development when UA and PA have been compiled at the correct spatial and temporal scales. Note that this is important because UA and PA varies across space and time. That is why we can't assume they are constant. Most of the time, maps have an accuracy compiled at national level, not at county and municipal level. SSB and CSO use these maps for county and municipal levels and ignore the fact that UA and PA should be different at county and municipal level due to spatial variations. This means that the areas reported at county and municipal level have not been corrected and are thus eith under or overestimated.


## 6. References

NRK, 2024. Norge i rødt, hvitt og grått. https://www.nrk.no/dokumentar/xl/nrk-avslorer_-44.000-inngrep-i-norsk-natur-pa-fem-ar-1.16573560 

NRK, 2025. NRK avslører europeisk naturtap: Norge topper verstingliste [online]. NRK. URL https://www.nrk.no/dokumentar/xl/europa-fra-gront-til-gratt_-nordmenn-bygger-ned-mest-natur_-viser-europeisk-kartlegging-1.17577504 (accessed 2.20.26).

Venter, Z.S., Czúcz, B., Stange, E., Nowell, M.S., Simensen, T., Immerzeel, B., Barton, D.N., 2024. ‘Uncertainty audit’ for ecosystem accounting: Satellite-based ecosystem extent is biased without design-based area estimation and accuracy assessment. Ecosystem Services 66: 101599. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecoser.2024.101599

Venter, Z., 2025. Map of built-up expansion ("nedbygging") over Norway 2017-2024 with year of loss. Zenodo. https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.15130992


## 7. Import and prepare datasets
In this section, we show how to import the data through API. We also prepare them  for analyses. We first set up the path that will be used during the project. Users can change it to match their own path.
```{r path_setup1}
#| eval: true
#| code-summary: "Path setup"

# Path to the project folder
project_path <- here::here()

# Path to the functions folder within the project folder
functions_path <- here::here(project_path, "src/functions")
```

<!--# All the data import and preparation should be done in this chapter, before the analysis chapter. As a minimum, add code for importing data, and describe or show the data structure. You may also explain certain variables, what they represent, and their distributions -->

### 7.1 Map of municipalities in Norway - DT01
#### 7.1.1 Download & read DT01 from GISCO through an API
For DT03, the API process is much simpler as a package has been developed to read data from GISCO via an API: `giscoR`. We just need to use one function to cache the dataset in a temporary directory and read it in R.
```{r lau_down_data}
#| eval: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| code-summary: "DT01 API download"

# Create a temporary directory to store the cache
temp_dir <- tempdir()

# Download the dataset
lau_no <- gisco_get_lau(year = "2024",
                        epsg = "3035",
                        cache = TRUE,
                        update_cache = TRUE,
                        cache_dir = temp_dir,
                        verbose = FALSE,
                        country = "NO",
                        gisco_id = NULL)

```

#### 7.1.2 Clean and format the data for analyses
We select the columns of relevance to our work and clean the topological and geometrical errors if any. Note that we also need to clean the `GISCO_ID`, which is the municipality number. 

```{r clean_lau}
#| eval: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| output: false
#| code-summary: "DT03 - general clean and format"

# We select the columns of interests
lau_no <- lau_no %>%
            select(GISCO_ID,
                   LAU_NAME) %>%
            mutate(GISCO_ID = sub("NO_", "", .$GISCO_ID)) %>%
            st_transform(., crs = "EPSG:25832") # project to ESPG : 25832

# Check for topological and geometrical errors
which(st_is_valid(lau_no) == FALSE) %>% length()
which(st_is_empty(lau_no) == TRUE) %>% length()

# Clean R environment
rm(list = setdiff(ls(), 
                  c("lau_no",
                    "project_path",
                    "functions_path")))
```

### 7.2 Recreation areas - DT02
#### 7.2.1 Download DT02 for Geonorge through an API
We download DT02 through an API from [Geonorge](https://kartkatalog.geonorge.no/metadata/friluftslivsomraader-kartlagte/91e31bb7-356f-4478-bcba-d5c2de6e91bc?search=friluftsliv). Downloading through API in Geonorge is not an easy process, hence we fully decompose the API download request to facilitate the download of other datasets by users if they wish to. Note however that datasets not publicly accessible cannot be downloaded via an API.

1. **Create a  <u>base url</u>**. The *base url* is the basic url that needs to be used when downloading data. It will be the same for all datasets. In Geonorge, users should go the their datsaset of interest and scroll to the bottom of the page to the section *Distribusjon*. There, there are several subsections called *Distribusjonstype*. They display the *base url* depending on where the users want to download the data from. In our case, we chose to download our data from Geonorge whose *base url* is https://geoapi.p.aks.miljodirektoratet.no/geonorge. Here we removed the last part of the url (/capabilities) as it refers to another concept explained in the point 3 below.

```{r import_rec_areas_api1}
#| eval: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| code-summary: "DT02 API download - base url"

# base url for downloading dataset in Geonorge through API
base_url <- "https://geoapi.p.aks.miljodirektoratet.no/geonorge"
```


2. **Identify the <u>dataset ID </u>**. The dataset ID is called an uuid. It can be found by going to the dataset's page on Geonorge, it is the long series of numbers and letters in the url of the page: ".../metadata/dataset_name/uuid"
```{r import_rec_areas_api2}
#| eval: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| code-summary: "DT02 API download - dataset ID"

# dataset specific ID from Geonorge
dataset_uuid <- "91e31bb7-356f-4478-bcba-d5c2de6e91bc"
```

3. **Request the dataset's <u>capabilities</u>**. When downloading data through an API, users have to choose what is called "capabilities". These refer to the settings that should be used when downloading the data such as geographic areas, raster or vector type, format etc. We need to first build an API request to know what these capabilities are before downloading the data.
```{r import_rec_areas_api3}
#| eval: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| code-summary: "DT02 API download - list capabilities"

# httr2:::req_perform() sends API request for dataset's capabilities
dt_cap <- request(sprintf("%s/capabilities/%s", base_url, dataset_uuid)) %>% # create request
            req_headers("Accept" = "application/json") %>% # create request
            req_perform()


# Read the JSON response of the server listing the capabilities
cap_all <- resp_body_json(dt_cap, simplifyVector = TRUE)
```


4. **Choose the dataset's <u>capabilities</u> of relevance**. Now that we have the list of all the dataset's capabilities, we can choose those we want to download the dataset with. We are interested in three capabilities: projection, format, area and order. *Note: (i) order is the url to be used to order the dataset to the server; (ii) in the code below, the "rel" column just indicates what the link "href" refers to, meaning project, format, area, order etc. The url to use are in the "href" column; (iii) four projections can be chosen but in reality, only two are available for Norway as a country: ESPG:25833 and WGS84. Trying the others codes will lead to an error. (iv) we just read "http://rel.geonorge.no/download/area" as it has the area together withe project and format. So we don't need to read them all separately*
```{r import_rec_areas_api4}
#| eval: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| code-summary: "DT02 API download - choose capabilities"

# Extract the capablities into a dataframe so it's easier to choose from
cap_choice <- request(cap_all$`_links`[cap_all$`_links`$rel == "http://rel.geonorge.no/download/area", "href"]) %>%
                req_perform() %>%
                resp_body_json()

# Separate each capabilities: project, format and area
projection <- cap_choice[[1]]$projections[[1]] # ESPG:25833

format <- cap_choice[[1]]$formats[[2]]$name # FILEGDB

area <- cap_choice[[1]] # Norway with Svalbard

order_url <- cap_all$`_links`[cap_all$`_links`$rel == "http://rel.geonorge.no/download/order", "href"]

```


5. **Create an <u>order</u>**. Here we create the API request to order the data from the server. In our case, the server responds with a downloadable link that we can then use. Every server has its own JSON request structure, it is important to go on the [API help page of Geonorge](https://nedlasting.geonorge.no/Help) to use the correct structure. As we use a POST API order, we need to go to the [POST API oder page](https://nedlasting.geonorge.no/Help/Api/POST-api-order) to figure it out.
```{r import_rec_areas_api5}
#| eval: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| code-summary: "DT02 API download - order the data"

# Create the order by listing the dataset id and capabilities of relevance
order_body <- list(
  downloadAsBundle = FALSE,
  email = "sylvie.clappe@nina.no",
  orderLines = list(
    list(
      areas = list(
        list(
          code = area$code,  
          name = area$name,
          type = area$type
        )
      ),
      formats = list(
        list(name = format)
      ),
      metadataUuid = dataset_uuid,
      projections = list(
        list(
          code = projection$code,
          name = projection$name,
          codespace = projection$codespace
        )
      )
    )
  )
)

# Send the order to the server
order_req <- request(order_url) %>% # create request
                req_body_json(order_body) %>% # create request
                req_headers("Accept" = "application/json") %>% # create request
                req_perform() # send request

# Read the order information
order_info <- resp_body_json(order_req)
```

6. **Download the data**. Now that we have the downloadable link, we just have the use it to download the dataset in R. *Note: we download the data in a temporary file which can then be deleted.*
```{r import_rec_areas_api6}
#| eval: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| code-summary: "DT02 API download - download and read the data"

# URL to download the data
down_url <- order_info$files[[1]]$downloadUrl

# Create a temporary directory to store the dataset .zip and unzipped
temp_dir <- tempdir()

# Create a temporary file to store the dataset .zip
destination_tmp <- tempfile(fileext = ".zip", tmpdir = temp_dir)

# Download the .zip file containing the dataset
download.file(down_url, destfile = destination_tmp, mode = "wb")

# Unzip the .zip
unzip(destination_tmp, exdir = temp_dir)

# Use "\\.gdb$" pattern to identify the file to read
gdb_file <- list.files(temp_dir, pattern = "\\.gdb$", full.names = TRUE)

```


#### 7.2.2 Read DT02
Now that the dataset has been downloaded in a temporary file, we can read it in R and clean the R environment. Once the analyses are finished, we will remove the temporary file containing the data.
```{r read_rec_area}
#| eval: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| code-summary: "DT02 - read the data"

# Read data
rec_no <- st_read(gdb_file)

# Clean R environment
rm(list = setdiff(ls(), 
                  c("rec_no",
                    "lau_no",
                    "project_path",
                    "functions_path")))
```

#### 7.2.3 Clean and format the data for analyses
We first need to select the column of relevance for our work and clean the topological and geometrical errors if any.

```{r clean_rec_area1}
#| eval: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| output: false
#| code-summary: "DT02 - clean"

# Select columns of relevance
rec_areas <- rec_no %>%
              select(fylkesnr,
                     kommune,
                     kartleggingsår,
                     kartlagtStatus,
                     identifikasjon_lokalid,
                     områdeverdi)

# Check for topological and geometrical errors
which(st_is_valid(rec_areas) == FALSE) %>% length()
which(st_is_empty(rec_areas) == TRUE) %>% length()

# Correct invalid geometries
rec_cln <- st_make_valid(rec_areas)

# Check correction worked
which(st_is_valid(rec_cln) == FALSE) %>% length()
which(st_is_empty(rec_cln) == TRUE) %>% length()

# Clean a typo
rec_cln$kartleggingsår[rec_cln$kartleggingsår == "2921"] <- "2021"

```

DT02 includes both terrestrial and marine outdoor recreation areas. That is why we first did a spatial intersection between DT02 and DT01 to restrict the analyses to terrestrial outdoor recreation areas only. The reason for this is that DT03 only encompasses encroachment within terrestrial areas. Note that we had to first dissolve municipal boundaries of DT01 as we are only interested in correcting the coastal boundaries of outdoor recreation areas.

```{r clean_rec_area2}
#| eval: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| output: false
#| code-summary: "DT02 - restrict to terrestrial recreation areas only"

# Check the CRS of both DT01 and DT02
st_crs(lau_no) == st_crs(rec_cln)


# Re-project rec_cln into ESPG : 25832
rcl_32 <- st_transform(rec_cln, crs = st_crs(lau_no))

# Perform spatial intersection to have terrestrial recreation areas only
rcl_32_tr <- lau_no %>%
              st_union() %>%
              st_intersection(rcl_32, .) %>%
              mutate(area_rec_m2 = unclass(st_area(.)), .after = områdeverdi)

```

The last step of formatting for the recreation data is to correct the year of mapping of municipalities with several and only report the most recent year. Except for Oslo, where the different district mapped their recreation areas in different years, the other municipalities should only have one year. This is in fact not the case in the recreation dataset. 196 municipalities have polygons appearing for more than one year. This is because they updated their polygons by adding new ones. So we need to take into account this year of update as the final mapping year.


```{r clean_rec_area3}
#| eval: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| output: false
#| code-summary: "DT02 - correct years of mapping"

# Select municipalities have more than one year
several_yr_mun <- rcl_32_tr %>%
                    st_drop_geometry() %>%
                    select(kommune,
                           kartleggingsår) %>%
                    filter(!kommune == "0301") %>%
                    group_by(kommune) %>%
                    summarise(count_yr = n_distinct(kartleggingsår)) %>%
                    filter(count_yr >1) %>%
                    ungroup() 

# Select the most recent year of update as the mapping year
update_yr_mun <- rcl_32_tr %>%
                    st_drop_geometry() %>%
                    select(kommune,
                           kartleggingsår) %>%
                    filter(kommune %in% as.vector(several_yr_mun$kommune)) %>%
                    group_by(kommune) %>%
                    arrange(kartleggingsår, .by_group = TRUE) %>% 
                    slice_tail(n = 1) %>%
                    ungroup() %>%
                    rename(kartleggingsår_updated = kartleggingsår)
                    
# Replace all the mapping years of the 103 municipalities in update_yr_mun by their last year of update
rcl_final <- rcl_32_tr %>%
                left_join(., update_yr_mun, by = "kommune") %>%
                mutate(kartleggingsår = if_else(!is.na(kartleggingsår_updated), 
                                                kartleggingsår_updated, 
                                                kartleggingsår)) %>%
                select(!kartleggingsår_updated)


# Clean R environment
rm(list = setdiff(ls(), 
                  c("rcl_32_tr",
                    "lau_no",
                    "rcl_final",
                    "project_path",
                    "functions_path")))


```


### 7.3. Land take areas - DT03
#### 7.3.1 Download DT03 from Zenodo through an API
The process is very similar than for Geonorge. Instead of going through all the steps above, we created a function that users can use to retrieve the downloadable *content* link to the data. We could have used `zen4R` package but we decided to create our own function to read the data from Zenodo directly instead of downloading them locally before reading them.
```{r enc_link}
#| eval: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| code-summary: "DT03 API download"

# Call for the function that will read the data through an API
source(paste0(functions_path,"/api_zenodo.R"))

# Retrieve downloadable link to the data
encroach_link <- api_zenodo(zenodo_url = "https://zenodo.org/records/15130992")

```

#### 7.2.2 Read DT03
Now that we have the downloadable link, we can read the data in R and clean the R environment.
```{r enc_read}
#| eval: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| code-summary: "DT03 - read"

encroach_dt <- st_read(encroach_link)

```

#### 7.3.3 Clean and format the data for analyses
All columns are of relevance to our work but we need to clean the topological and geometrical errors if any.

```{r clean_enc}
#| eval: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| output: false
#| code-summary: "DT03 - clean"

# Check for topological and geometrical errors
which(st_is_valid(encroach_dt) == FALSE) %>% length()
which(st_is_empty(encroach_dt) == TRUE) %>% length()

# Clean R environment
rm(list = setdiff(ls(), 
                  c("rcl_32_tr",
                    "lau_no",
                    "rcl_final",
                    "encroach_dt",
                    "project_path",
                    "functions_path")))

```

The data are all clear and do not need further corrections.


### 7.4 Naturtyper - verdsatte - DT04
#### 7.4.1 Download DT04 for Geonorge through an API
We use the same protocol as for DT01. This time, we created a function for it.
```{r import_natur_api1}
#| eval: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| code-summary: "DT04 API download"
# Call for the function that will read the data through an API
source(paste0(functions_path,"/api_geonorge.R"))

# Retrieve and download the data
natur_link <- api_geonorge(geonorge_url = "https://kartkatalog.geonorge.no/metadata/naturtyper-verdsatte/64cbb884-a19d-4356-a114-380cfe4a7314",
                              email_address = "sylvie.clappe@nina.no")

```

#### 7.4.2 Read DT04
Now that the dataset has been downloaded in a temporary file, we can read it in R and clean the R environment. Once the analyses are finished, we will remove the temporary file containing the data.
```{r read_nature}
#| eval: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| code-summary: "DT04 - read the data"

# Read data
naturtyper <- st_read(natur_link[grepl("Naturtyper", natur_link)])
```

#### 7.4.3 Clean and format the data for analyses
We first need to select the column of relevance for our work and clean the topological and geometrical errors if any. The two major issues we have with these data are the spatial and temporal coverages. Spatial coverage because the data do not cover the entire country of Norway. This means that it's not because we don't record one a nature type as occurring that it is indeed not occurring, it might just not have been sampled yet. Also, the nature types have been recorded only once for a wide temporal coverage: from 1905 to 2025. Given that our encroachment data cover 2017-2024, we decided to keep only the data that have been recorded in 2015 or after. We consider that a record in 2025 doesn't mean that the type was not present before. We also assume that nature types are relatively stable and have not change since 2017. We also removed all nature types whose year of report was not stated. This made us keep 69.5% of the original dataset, which is still very good for analyses.

In our report, we define "specially important nature" as the nature types within DT03 that are classified as "stor" and "svært stor" verdi. So we need to filter for them.

*Note: we restrict the coverage to 2025, which means that we filter out the polygons from 2026. This was done to be consistent with the figures in the NINA report we published but can of course be removed!*

```{r clean_natur}
#| eval: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| output: false
#| code-summary: "DT04 - clean and format"

# Select columns of relevance
natur_types <- naturtyper %>%
                mutate(year_natur_recorded = substr(.$registreringsdato, 1, 4)) %>%
                filter(verdikategori %in% c("Svært stor verdi",
                                            "Stor verdi")) %>%
                select(naturtype,
                       year_natur_recorded,
                       kommunenummer)

# Check for topological and geometrical errors
which(st_is_valid(natur_types) == FALSE) %>% length()
which(st_is_empty(natur_types) == TRUE) %>% length()

# Correct invalid geometries
natur_cln <- st_make_valid(natur_types)

# Check correction worked
which(st_is_valid(natur_cln) == FALSE) %>% length()
which(st_is_empty(natur_cln) == TRUE) %>% length()

# Clean typos in years
natur_cln$year_natur_recorded[natur_cln$year_natur_recorded == "2102"] <- "2012"
natur_cln$year_natur_recorded[natur_cln$year_natur_recorded == "2913"] <- "2013"


# Remove nature types that have been recorded before 2010
natur_cln <- natur_cln %>%
              filter(year_natur_recorded > 2009) %>%
              filter(year_natur_recorded < 2026) %>%
              filter(!is.na(year_natur_recorded))
  

# Clean R environment
rm(list = setdiff(ls(), 
                  c("lau_no",
                    "rcl_32_tr",
                    "rcl_final",
                    "encroach_dt",
                    "natur_cln",
                    "lau_no",
                    "project_path",
                    "functions_path")))

```

## 8. Spatial units
All of our accounts have been compiled for Norway (Ecosystem Accounting Area) and broken down at municipal and county levels. As explained in section 3.1, the "ecosystem" of interest in ENCREC_001-002 is "land take". This means that our Basic Spatial Units (BSU) are "land take" polygons in ENCREC_001-002. For ENCREC_005-006, the BSU was "specially important nature" polygons. The Ecosystem Asset (EA) is "land take" for ENCREC_001-002 and "specially important nature types" for ENCREC_005-006.

For ENCREC_003-004 is a little bit different and the BSU is the outdoor recreation areas polygons, while the EA would be outdoor recreation areas. ENCREC_003-004 are not reported in our NINA Report and is just a way of seeing the data differently.

## 9. Analyses
### 9.1 ENCREC_001 to ENCREC_004
Both the analyses for ENCREC_001 to ENCREC_004 rely on spatial intersections between DT02 and DT03. Also, note that we re-calculate the areas of the encroachment polygons after the intersection with DT02 has been performed.

*Note: some municipalities had no encroachments detected in certain years. In these cases, the year where 0 encroachment was detected did not not appear. We thus had to add it manually for all municipalities. In these cases, we reported the uncertainty of the year of encroachment.*

```{r encrec001234_analyses}
#| eval: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| output: false
#| code-summary: "ENCREC_001 and 002 - intersection"

# Check the CRS of both DT02 and DT03
st_crs(rcl_final) == st_crs(encroach_dt)

# Perform spatial intersection
encrec_raw <- st_intersection(rcl_final, encroach_dt) %>%
                mutate(area_inter_m2 = unclass(st_area(.)))

# Add missing years for accounting. Set the area as 0 for the missing year.
encrec <- encrec_raw %>%
            st_drop_geometry() %>%
            complete(nesting(fylkesnr, kommune, områdeverdi), year = 2018:2024, fill = list(id = "NA",
                                                                                area = 0,
                                                                                area_inter_m2 = 0))

# Clean R environment
rm(list = setdiff(ls(), 
                  c("lau_no",
                    "encroach_dt",
                    "natur_cln",
                    "project_path",
                    "functions_path",
                    "rcl_final",
                    "encrec_raw",
                    "encrec")))

```
### 9.2 ENCREC_005 and ENCREC_006
For ENCREC_005 and ENCRREC_006, we need a spatial intersection between DT04 and `encrec` as we are interested in the nature types that have been lost due to encroachment within recreation areas. Here we have to note though that the only 1,5% of the specially important nature occur in the encroachment of outdoor recreation areas.

```{r encrec0056_analyses}
#| eval: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| output: false
#| code-summary: "ENCREC_003 - intersection"

# Check the CRS of both encrec and DT04
st_crs(encrec_raw) == st_crs(natur_cln)

# Re-project natur_cln into ESPG : 25832
natur_32 <- st_transform(natur_cln, crs = st_crs(encrec_raw))

# Check CRS of both datasets are the same now
st_crs(natur_32) == st_crs(encrec_raw)

# Perform spatial intersection
encnatur_raw <- encrec_raw %>%
                 select(fylkesnr,
                        kommune,
                        kartleggingsår,
                        områdeverdi,
                        id,
                        year,
                        area_inter_m2) %>%
                 rename(area_encrec = area_inter_m2,
                        year_encrec = year) %>%
                 st_intersection(., natur_32) %>%
                 mutate(area_naturtyper_m2 = unclass(st_area(.)))

encnatur <- encnatur_raw %>%
            st_drop_geometry() %>%
            select(!c(kommunenummer, area_encrec, id)) %>%
            complete(nesting(fylkesnr, kommune, naturtype), 
                     year_encrec = 2018:2024,
                     fill = list(area = 0,
                                 area_naturtyper_m2 = 0)) %>%
            mutate(year_natur_recorded = coalesce(as.integer(year_natur_recorded), year_encrec))

# Clean R environment
rm(list = setdiff(ls(), 
                  c("lau_no",
                    "encroach_dt",
                    "project_path",
                    "functions_path",
                    "rcl_final",
                    "natur_32",
                    "encrec_raw",
                    "encrec",
                    "encnatur_raw",
                    "encnatur")))

```

### 9.3 Uncertainty analyses
Our traffic-light uncertainty required to steps:
1. Attribute a traffic-light uncertainty at the polygon level. For this we used our lookup table developed in our NINA Rapport 2754; 

2. Derive a municipal / county traffic-light uncertainty based on the frequency of the traffic-light uncertainty at polygon-level (step 1 above). We basically created a frequency table of all the traffic-light color uncertainties from the polygons and derived the municipal / county traffic-light color uncertainty based the most predominant polygons' traffic-light colors. See NINA Rapport 2754 for more information.


First we attributed a colored-uncertainty to all polygons. `unc_0013_idv` and `unc_0024_idv`serve for both ENCREC_001 and ENCREC_003, and ENCREC_002 and ENCREC_004. This is because the same information is presented differently in both ENCREC_001/ENCREC_003 and ENCREC_002/ENCREC_004.

```{r recacc_uncertainty_1}
#| eval: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| output: false
#| code-summary: "Step 1 - attribute colored-uncertainty to all polygons"

# Load necessary functions
source(paste0(functions_path,"/add_uncertainty_individual.R"))

# Uncertainty RECACC_001
unc_0013_idv <- encrec %>%
                select(kommune, year, kartleggingsår) %>%
                mutate(uncertainty_source2 = apply(., 1, function(x) {add_uncertainty_individual(data = x, source2 = TRUE, yrsource2 = c("year", "kartleggingsår"))}))


unc_0024_idv <- encrec %>%
                    filter(områdeverdi %in% c("viktigFriluftslivsområde", 
                                              "sværtViktigFriluftslivsområde")) %>%
                select(kommune, year, kartleggingsår) %>%
                mutate(uncertainty_source2 = apply(., 1, function(x) {add_uncertainty_individual(data = x, source2 = TRUE, yrsource2 = c("year", "kartleggingsår"))}))

# Uncertainty ENCACCC_005
unc_005_idv <- encnatur %>%
                select(kommune, year_encrec, year_natur_recorded, naturtype) %>%
                mutate(uncertainty_source2 = apply(., 1, function(x) {add_uncertainty_individual(data = x, source2 = TRUE, yrsource2 = c("year_encrec", "year_natur_recorded"))}))

# Uncertainty ENCACCC_006
unc_006_idv <- encnatur %>%
                filter(områdeverdi %in% c("viktigFriluftslivsområde", 
                                          "sværtViktigFriluftslivsområde")) %>%
                select(kommune, year_encrec, year_natur_recorded, naturtype) %>%
                mutate(uncertainty_source2 = apply(., 1, function(x) {add_uncertainty_individual(data = x, source2 = TRUE, yrsource2 = c("year_encrec", "year_natur_recorded"))}))

# Clean R environment
rm(list = setdiff(ls(), 
                  c("lau_no",
                    "encroach_dt",
                    "project_path",
                    "functions_path",
                    "rcl_final",
                    "natur_32",
                    "encrec_raw",
                    "encrec",
                    "encnatur_raw",
                    "encnatur",
                    "unc_0013_idv",
                    "unc_0024_idv",
                    "unc_005_idv",
                    "unc_006_idv")))

```

Now we can concatenate the traffic-light uncertainty from polygon level to municipal and county levels. As we haven't calculated the extent accounts yet, we won't derive the the traffic-light uncertainty at municipal / county level. We prefer to wait until we build the accounts as we need to add changes in extent as well. It is easier to do it in the end.

*Note: for "unc_005_sum" and "unc_006_sum", we only concatenate per habitat types as the accounts present a total extent of the specially important nature lost over the total accounting period 2018-2024.*

```{r encacc_uncertainty_2}
#| eval: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| output: false
#| code-summary: "Pre-Step 2 - concatenate the polygin uncertainty from Step 1 at municipal / county levels"

# Call function to concatenate
source(paste0(functions_path,"/concatenate_individual_uncertainty.R"))

# Concatenate for RECACC_001 and RECACC_003
unc_0013_sum <- concatenate_fn(unc_0013_idv,
                              col_column_names = "uncertainty_source2", 
                              kom_num = "kommune",
                              accounting_year = "year")

# Concatenate for RECACC_002 and RECACC_004
unc_0024_sum <- concatenate_fn(unc_0024_idv,
                              col_column_names = "uncertainty_source2", 
                              kom_num = "kommune",
                              accounting_year = "year")


# Concatenate for RECACC_005
unc_005_sum <- concatenate_fn(unc_005_idv,
                              col_column_names = "uncertainty_source2", 
                              kom_num = "kommune",
                              habitat_col = "naturtype")

# Concatenate for RECACC_006
unc_006_sum <- concatenate_fn(unc_006_idv,
                              col_column_names = "uncertainty_source2",
                              kom_num = "kommune",
                              habitat_col = "naturtype")


# Clean R environment
rm(list = setdiff(ls(), 
                  c("lau_no",
                    "encroach_dt",
                    "project_path",
                    "functions_path",
                    "rcl_final",
                    "natur_32",
                    "encrec_raw",
                    "encrec",
                    "encnatur_raw",
                    "encnatur",
                    "unc_0013_idv",
                    "unc_0024_idv",
                    "unc_005_idv",
                    "unc_006_idv",
                    "unc_0013_idv",
                    "unc_0024_idv",
                    "unc_0013_sum",
                    "unc_0024_sum",
                    "unc_005_sum",
                    "unc_006_sum")))

```


## 10. Results - Thematic Extent Accounts: land take in recreation areas
Now we can built the accounting tables and maps based on the spatial intersection we performed in the previous section. We chose to build the thematic accounts over the 2018-2024 period. Maps will be created only for the closing (2024) year of the total accounting period 2018-2024. **Only municipalities which have mapped their recreation areas are present in these accounts.**

All recreation areas have been mapped in different years ranging from 2010 to 2018. We decided to not take this difference in our analyses and the subsequent presentation of the accounts. This means that we consider the mapped recreation areas as homogeneous across the years. Our assumption for this is that the year of mapping does not indicate that the area mapped was not a recreation area before it was mapped. For example, we consider that recreation areas only mapped in 2025 should be part of the accounts as we assume that people would already use these areas to recreate in 2018, despite them not having been officially mapped in 2018 by municipalities. This assumption was used to <u>build the thematic account </u>. Nevertheless, we expect that a recreation areas mapped in 2010 might present some variations overtime and would present changes in its area in 2024. We took this possible temporal discrepancy into account in the uncertainty presented alongside the results.


### 10.1 Accounting tables

#### 10.1.1 ENCREC_001 and ENCREC_002: Extent accounts of land take in recreation areas
Here we present the extent accounts table for land take areas between 2018 and 2024 for recreation areas within each municipality in Norway. We compile the extent of these land take areas in all recreation areas (ENCREC_001), but also only in recreation areas classified as important and very important (ENCREC_002). These accounts present both the yearly area of land take and the cumulative area of land take over the years. We also present a total across all municipalities in Norway which mapped their recreation areas.

Note that only the municipalities which have mapped their outdoors areas appear in the table below.

```{r res_acc_tables1}
#| eval: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| output: true
#| code-summary: "ENCREC_001 and ENCREC_002- Create accounting table per municipality"

## Call function to add change in areas 2018-2024
source(paste0(functions_path, "/add_change_encroachment_area.R"))

## Call function to derive uncertainty at municipal / fylke level
source(paste0(functions_path, "/add_uncertainty_global.R"))

## Call function to concatenate
source(paste0(functions_path,"/concatenate_individual_uncertainty.R"))

## Import LAU dataset to then join the name of the municipalities
kom_navn <- lau_no %>%
              st_drop_geometry() %>%
              select(GISCO_ID,
                     LAU_NAME)

## ENCREC_001 and 002 accounting tables
# Accounts for all municipalities
encrec_001_kom <- encrec %>%
                    select(fylkesnr,
                           kommune,
                           year,
                           area_inter_m2) %>%
                    group_by(fylkesnr, kommune, year) %>%
                    summarise(area_dekar_yearly = round2(sum(area_inter_m2)/1000, 2)) %>%
                    mutate(area_dekar_cumulative = round2(cumsum(area_dekar_yearly), 2)) %>%
                    left_join(., kom_navn, join_by("kommune" == "GISCO_ID")) %>%
                    relocate(c(LAU_NAME), .after = "kommune") %>%
                    mutate(indicator_id = "ENCREC_001", .before = "fylkesnr")

encrec_002_kom <- encrec %>%
                    filter(områdeverdi %in% c("viktigFriluftslivsområde", 
                                              "sværtViktigFriluftslivsområde")) %>%
                    select(fylkesnr,
                           kommune,
                           year,
                           area_inter_m2) %>%
                    group_by(fylkesnr, kommune, year) %>%
                    summarise(area_dekar_yearly = round2(sum(area_inter_m2)/1000, 2)) %>%
                    mutate(area_dekar_cumulative = round2(cumsum(area_dekar_yearly), 2)) %>%
                    left_join(., kom_navn, join_by("kommune" == "GISCO_ID")) %>%
                    relocate(c(LAU_NAME), .after = "kommune") %>%
                    mutate(indicator_id = "ENCREC_002", .before = "fylkesnr")

# Add uncertainties to encacc_001_kom
encrec_001_kom_un <- encrec_001_kom %>%
                      left_join(., unc_0013_sum, by = c("kommune", "year")) %>%
                      add_uncertainty_global(., 
                                             uncertainty_col_name = "unc_concat_full", 
                                             kom_num = "kommune", 
                                             accounting_year = "year") %>%
                      select(!unc_concat_full)

encrec_002_kom_un <- encrec_002_kom %>%
                      left_join(., unc_0024_sum, by = c("kommune", "year")) %>%
                      add_uncertainty_global(., 
                                             uncertainty_col_name = "unc_concat_full", 
                                             kom_num = "kommune", 
                                             accounting_year = "year") %>%
                      select(!unc_concat_full)


# Total across all municipalities
encrec_001_tot <- encrec_001_kom %>%
                    group_by(year) %>%
                    summarise(area_dekar_yearly = sum(area_dekar_yearly)) %>%
                    mutate(area_dekar_cumulative = round2(cumsum(area_dekar_yearly), 2)) %>%
                    mutate(indicator_id = "ENCREC_001",
                           fylkesnr = "00",
                           kommune = "0000",
                           LAU_NAME = "Totalt", .before = year)

encrec_002_tot <- encrec_002_kom %>%
                    group_by(year) %>%
                    summarise(area_dekar_yearly = sum(area_dekar_yearly)) %>%
                    mutate(area_dekar_cumulative = round2(cumsum(area_dekar_yearly), 2)) %>%
                    mutate(indicator_id = "ENCREC_002",
                           fylkesnr = "00",
                           kommune = "0000",
                           LAU_NAME = "Totalt", .before = year)
                     

# Add uncertainty to encacc_001_tot and encrec_002_tot
encrec_001_tot_un <-  unc_0013_idv %>%
                        mutate(national = "0000") %>%
                        concatenate_fn(.,
                                       col_column_names = "uncertainty_source2",
                                       kom_num = "national",
                                       accounting_year = "year") %>%
                        add_uncertainty_global(., 
                                               kom_num = "national",
                                               uncertainty_col_name = "unc_concat_full",
                                               accounting_year = "year") %>%
                      ungroup() %>%
                      select(!c(unc_concat_full,
                                national,
                                year)) %>%
                      cbind(encrec_001_tot, .)

encrec_002_tot_un <-  unc_0024_idv %>%
                        mutate(national = "0000") %>%
                        concatenate_fn(.,
                                       col_column_names = "uncertainty_source2",
                                       kom_num = "national",
                                       accounting_year = "year") %>%
                        add_uncertainty_global(., 
                                               kom_num = "national",
                                               uncertainty_col_name = "unc_concat_full",
                                               accounting_year = "year") %>%
                      ungroup() %>%
                      select(!c(unc_concat_full,
                                national,
                                year)) %>%
                      cbind(encrec_002_tot, .)


# Add change to all tables
encrec_001_kom_un$year <- as.character(encrec_001_kom_un$year)

encrec_001_kom_un <- encrec_001_kom_un %>%
                      group_split(kommune) %>% 
                      map(~ add_change_encroachment_area(., 
                                                         year_column = "year",
                                                         area1 = "area_dekar_cumulative")) %>% 
                      compact() %>%
                      bind_rows()

encrec_002_kom_un$year <- as.character(encrec_002_kom_un$year)

encrec_002_kom_un <- encrec_002_kom_un %>%
                      group_split(kommune) %>% 
                      map(~ add_change_encroachment_area(., 
                                                         year_column = "year",
                                                         area1 = "area_dekar_cumulative")) %>% 
                      compact() %>%
                      bind_rows()


encrec_001_tot_un$year <- as.character(encrec_001_tot_un$year)

encrec_001_tot_un <- add_change_encroachment_area(encrec_001_tot_un, 
                                                  year_column = "year",
                                                  area1 = "area_dekar_cumulative") %>% 
                      compact() %>%
                      bind_rows()


encrec_002_tot_un$year <- as.character(encrec_002_tot_un$year)

encrec_002_tot_un <- add_change_encroachment_area(encrec_002_tot_un, 
                                                  year_column = "year",
                                                  area1 = "area_dekar_cumulative") %>% 
                      compact() %>%
                      bind_rows()


# Correct uncertainty for global_yearly_uncertainty when accounting year is "Change 2018-2024"
encrec_001_kom_un[which(encrec_001_kom_un$year == "Change 2018-2024"), "global_yearly_uncertainty"] <- "NA"
encrec_002_kom_un[which(encrec_002_kom_un$year == "Change 2018-2024"), "global_yearly_uncertainty"] <- "NA"
encrec_001_tot_un[which(encrec_001_tot_un$year == "Change 2018-2024"), "global_yearly_uncertainty"] <- "NA"
encrec_002_tot_un[which(encrec_002_tot_un$year == "Change 2018-2024"), "global_yearly_uncertainty"] <- "NA"

# Translate "Change 2018-2024" in Norwegian
encrec_001_kom_un[which(encrec_001_kom_un$year == "Change 2018-2024"), "year"] <- "Endring 2018-2024"
encrec_002_kom_un[which(encrec_002_kom_un$year == "Change 2018-2024"), "year"] <- "Endring 2018-2024"
encrec_001_tot_un[which(encrec_001_tot_un$year == "Change 2018-2024"), "year"] <- "Endring 2018-2024"
encrec_002_tot_un[which(encrec_002_tot_un$year == "Change 2018-2024"), "year"] <- "Endring 2018-2024"
                    
# Final accounting table               
encrec_0012_acc <- bind_rows(encrec_001_kom_un,
                             encrec_001_tot_un, 
                             encrec_002_kom_un,
                             encrec_002_tot_un)

encrec_0012_acc$fylkesnr[which(encrec_0012_acc$fylkesnr == "00")] <- "Totalt"
encrec_0012_acc$kommune[which(encrec_0012_acc$kommune == "0000")] <- "Totalt"

colnames(encrec_0012_acc) <- c("Indikatorens navn",
                               "Fylke nummer",
                               "Kommune nummer",
                               "Kommune navn",
                               "Regnskapsår",
                               "Årlig areal i dekar",
                               "Kumulativ areal i dekar",
                               "Usikkerhet for årlig areal",
                               "Usikkerhet for kumulativ areal")

# Clean R environment
rm(list = setdiff(ls(), 
                  c("lau_no",
                    "encroach_dt",
                    "project_path",
                    "functions_path",
                    "rcl_final",
                    "natur_32",
                    "encrec_raw",
                    "encrec",
                    "encnatur_raw",
                    "encnatur",
                    "unc_0013_idv",
                    "unc_0024_idv",
                    "unc_005_idv",
                    "unc_006_idv",
                    "unc_0013_idv",
                    "unc_0024_idv",
                    "unc_0013_sum",
                    "unc_0024_sum",
                    "unc_005_sum",
                    "unc_006_sum",
                    "kom_navn",
                    "add_change_encroachment_area",
                    "add_uncertainty_global",
                    "concatenate_fn",
                    "add_fylke.R",
                    "encrec_0012_acc")))


# Display the table dynamically
datatable(encrec_0012_acc,
          extensions = 'Buttons',
          filter = list(position = 'top', clear = TRUE), 
          options = list( dom = 'Bfrtip',
                          buttons = c('copy', 'csv', 'excel', 'pdf', 'print'),
                          columnDefs = list(list(targets = c(which(colnames(encrec_0012_acc) == "Usikkerhet for årlig areal"),
                                                             which(colnames(encrec_0012_acc) == "Usikkerhet for kumulativ areal")),
                                                 visible = FALSE))),
          caption = htmltools::tags$caption(style = "caption-side: top; text-align: center; font-size: 1.5em;",
                                            htmltools::strong("Tabell 1 – Tematiske arealregnskap: areal av all nedbygging i friluftslivomeråder i kommunner i perioden 2018–2024."))) %>%
    formatCurrency(
    columns = c("Årlig areal i dekar", "Kumulativ areal i dekar"),
    currency = "",                
    interval = 3,                 
    mark = " " ,
    dec.mark = ",") %>%
  ## Example: color Årlig areal i dekar by var1
  formatStyle(
    "Årlig areal i dekar",
    backgroundColor = styleEqual(
      c("Green",   "Yellow",    "Orange",    "Red", "NA"),
      c("#94F4B6", "#FFF085", "#FFC68A", "#FF8A8A", "")
    ),
    valueColumns = "Usikkerhet for årlig areal"           # column used for the intervals
  ) %>%
  ## Example: color Kumulativ areal i dekar by var2
  formatStyle(
    "Kumulativ areal i dekar",
    backgroundColor = styleEqual(
      c("Green",   "Yellow",    "Orange",    "Red"),
      c("#94F4B6", "#FFF085", "#FFC68A", "#FF8A8A")
    ),
    valueColumns = "Usikkerhet for kumulativ areal"           # column used for the intervals
  )

```

```{r res_acc_tables2}
#| eval: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| output: true
#| code-summary: "ENCREC_001 and ENCREC_002- Create accounting table per county"

# Call function to add county names and numbers
source(paste0(functions_path, "/add_fylke.R"))

# Create the accounting table for ENCREC_001 and ENCREC_002 based on encrec_0012_acc
encrec_001_fy <- encrec_0012_acc %>%
                  filter(!Regnskapsår == "Endring 2018-2024") %>%
                  filter(!`Kommune navn` == "Totalt") %>%
                  filter(`Indikatorens navn` == "ENCREC_001") %>%
                  group_by(`Indikatorens navn`, `Fylke nummer`, Regnskapsår) %>%
                  summarise(yearly_area = sum(`Årlig areal i dekar`)) %>%
                  mutate(cumulative_area = cumsum(yearly_area))

encrec_002_fy <- encrec_0012_acc %>%
                  filter(!Regnskapsår == "Endring 2018-2024") %>%
                  filter(!`Kommune navn` == "Totalt") %>%
                  filter(`Indikatorens navn` == "ENCREC_002") %>%
                  group_by(`Indikatorens navn`, `Fylke nummer`, Regnskapsår) %>%
                  summarise(yearly_area = sum(`Årlig areal i dekar`)) %>%
                  mutate(cumulative_area = cumsum(yearly_area))


# Add uncertainties to encacc_001_fy and encacc_002_fy
encrec_001_fy_un <-  unc_0013_idv %>%
                      mutate(fylke = add_fylke(data = ., kommune_nummer = "kommune")[[1]], .before = kommune) %>%
                      select(!kommune) %>%
                      concatenate_fn(.,
                                     col_column_names = c("uncertainty_source2"),
                                     kom_num = "fylke",
                                     accounting_year = "year") %>%
                      add_uncertainty_global(., 
                                                      kom_num = "fylke",
                                                      uncertainty_col_name = "unc_concat_full",
                                                      accounting_year = "year") %>%
                      select(!unc_concat_full) %>%
                      mutate(year = as.character(year)) %>%
                      left_join(encrec_001_fy, ., join_by(`Fylke nummer` == "fylke", "Regnskapsår" == "year")) %>%
                      ungroup() %>%
                      mutate(fylke_name = add_fylke(data = ., fylke_nummer = "Fylke nummer"), .after = `Fylke nummer`)


encrec_002_fy_un <-  unc_0024_idv %>%
                      mutate(fylke = add_fylke(data = ., kommune_nummer = "kommune")[[1]], .before = kommune) %>%
                      select(!kommune) %>%
                      concatenate_fn(.,
                                     col_column_names = c("uncertainty_source2"),
                                     kom_num = "fylke",
                                     accounting_year = "year") %>%
                      add_uncertainty_global(., 
                                                      kom_num = "fylke",
                                                      uncertainty_col_name = "unc_concat_full",
                                                      accounting_year = "year") %>%
                      select(!unc_concat_full) %>%
                      mutate(year = as.character(year)) %>%
                      left_join(encrec_002_fy, ., join_by(`Fylke nummer` == "fylke", "Regnskapsår" == "year")) %>%
                      ungroup() %>%
                      mutate(fylke_name = add_fylke(data = ., fylke_nummer = "Fylke nummer"), .after = `Fylke nummer`)


# Add change to encrec_001_fy_un and encrec_001_fy_un
encrec_001_fy_unc <- encrec_001_fy_un %>%
                      group_split(`Fylke nummer`) %>% 
                      map(~ add_change_encroachment_area(., 
                                                         year_column = "Regnskapsår",
                                                         area1 = "cumulative_area")) %>% 
                      compact() %>%
                      bind_rows()


encrec_002_fy_unc <- encrec_002_fy_un %>%
                      group_split(`Fylke nummer`) %>% 
                      map(~ add_change_encroachment_area(., 
                                                         year_column = "Regnskapsår",
                                                         area1 = "cumulative_area")) %>% 
                      compact() %>%
                      bind_rows()


# Correct uncertainty for global_yearly_uncertainty when accounting year is "Change 2018-2024"
encrec_001_fy_unc[which(encrec_001_fy_unc$Regnskapsår == "Change 2018-2024"), "global_yearly_uncertainty"] <- "NA"
encrec_002_fy_unc[which(encrec_002_fy_unc$Regnskapsår == "Change 2018-2024"), "global_yearly_uncertainty"] <- "NA"

encrec_001_fy_unc[which(encrec_001_fy_unc$Regnskapsår == "Change 2018-2024"), "Regnskapsår"] <- "Endring 2018-2024"
encrec_002_fy_unc[which(encrec_002_fy_unc$Regnskapsår == "Change 2018-2024"), "Regnskapsår"] <- "Endring 2018-2024"

# Modify colnames
colnames(encrec_001_fy_unc) <- c("Indikatorens navn",
                                 "Fylke nummer",
                                 "Fylke navn",
                                 "Regnskapsår",
                                 "Årlig areal i dekar",
                                 "Kumulativ areal i dekar",
                                 "Usikkerhet for årlig areal",
                                 "Usikkerhet for kumulativ areal")

colnames(encrec_002_fy_unc) <- colnames(encrec_001_fy_unc)


# Add total across fylke
encrec_0012_fy_tot <- encrec_0012_acc %>%
                        filter(`Kommune navn` == "Totalt") %>% 
                        rename("Fylke navn" = "Kommune navn") %>%
                        select(!`Kommune nummer`)
                        
  
encrec_0012_fy_acc <- bind_rows(encrec_001_fy_unc,
                                encrec_002_fy_unc,
                                encrec_0012_fy_tot)


# Clean R environment
rm(list = setdiff(ls(), 
                  c("lau_no",
                    "encroach_dt",
                    "project_path",
                    "functions_path",
                    "rcl_final",
                    "natur_32",
                    "encrec_raw",
                    "encrec",
                    "encnatur_raw",
                    "encnatur",
                    "unc_0013_idv",
                    "unc_0024_idv",
                    "unc_005_idv",
                    "unc_006_idv",
                    "unc_0013_idv",
                    "unc_0024_idv",
                    "unc_0013_sum",
```
